info.name: Exercise 2 — Auth & Coins API Flow (STARTER)
info.description:
## Exercise 2: Postman API Collection — Auth & Coins Flow

| Detail     | Value          |
|------------|----------------|
| **Time**   | ~40 minutes    |
| **Points** | 20             |
| **Type**   | Code — Postman collection (run optional) |

---

## Why This Exercise Exists

We build API tests first in Postman before any UI automation exists. We want to see whether you can build a **reusable, chained, environment-aware** collection — not just save curl commands.

---

## How to Open This File

1. Open **Postman** (free, https://www.postman.com/downloads/)
2. Click **Import** → drop `exercises/exercise_2_postman_collection.json`
3. The collection appears in the left sidebar with 4 starter requests
4. Make your changes inside Postman, then click the collection → **Export** → save back over `exercises/exercise_2_postman_collection.json`

---

## Task

The starter collection has 4 request stubs hitting a hypothetical Laravel + Sanctum API. They currently have **no auth, no assertions, no variables, and no chaining**. Your job:

1. **Create an environment** (in Postman, then export to `answers/postman_env.json`) with: `base_url`, `email`, `password`, `auth_token`, `user_id`, `room_id`, `gift_id`
2. **Login request** — capture the bearer token into `{{auth_token}}` via a test script
3. **Add a collection-level pre-request script** that auto-attaches `Authorization: Bearer {{auth_token}}` to every request except `/login`
4. **Add response assertions to each request:**
   - Status code
   - Response time (< 1500ms)
   - JSON schema validation (use `pm.response.to.have.jsonSchema(...)` with your own schema)
   - At least one business-logic assertion per endpoint (e.g., "remaining_balance decreased by the charged amount")
5. **Chain the requests** so they share state: login → get user → join room → send gift → assert balance updated
6. **Add at least 2 negative tests** as a separate folder: expired token returns 401, send gift with invalid quantity returns 422

---

## Do I Need to Run It?

**No — we evaluate the collection as written.** There is no real API at `api.example.com`. We are grading your test scripts, assertions, schemas, env-var usage, pre-request logic, and chaining — all of which are visible in the collection JSON itself.

If you DO want to execute it end-to-end (strong bonus signal), you have three options:

- **Postman Mock Server** (built-in, free): right-click the collection → **Mock Collection** → Postman generates a `{{base_url}}` that returns example responses you define. ~5 min setup.
- **Mockoon** (https://mockoon.com, free desktop app): point `{{base_url}}` at `http://localhost:3000` and stub the routes.
- **Newman** (CLI): `npx newman run exercise_2_postman_collection.json -e answers/postman_env.json` — this will only succeed against a real or mock backend.

If you run it, screenshot the green test run and put it in `answers/screenshots/exercise_2_run.png`.

---

## Submission

1. Re-export the collection from Postman over `exercises/exercise_2_postman_collection.json`
2. Export your environment to `answers/postman_env.json`
3. (Optional) `answers/exercise_2_notes.md` explaining any decisions (schema-validation approach, naming, etc.)
4. (Optional) Screenshot in `answers/screenshots/exercise_2_run.png` if you actually ran it

---

## Evaluation Criteria

| Criterion | Points | What We Look For |
|-----------|--------|------------------|
| Token capture & chaining | 5 | Login token flows automatically into subsequent requests via env var |
| Assertion quality | 5 | Status + headers + body + business logic, not just status |
| Schema validation | 3 | Reusable JSON schemas, not hand-rolled property-by-property checks |
| Negative tests | 3 | Real auth/validation failures, not just "send wrong URL" |
| Environment & pre-request usage | 2 | No hard-coded URLs/tokens; collection-level auth handling |
| Documentation | 2 | Each request has a meaningful description |

---

## Important

- **No hard-coded tokens, IDs, or URLs** in requests. Everything that varies per environment goes in env vars.
- **AI will give you a flat list of requests with `pm.test('status is 200')`.** We can tell. Show real chaining and real schema validation.
- If you have deep experience with a different API testing tool (REST Assured, Karate, Bruno), you may submit in that tool instead — note why in `answers/exercise_2_notes.md`.

request.description: TODO (candidate): replace hard-coded URL/body with env vars. Capture the returned bearer token into {{auth_token}}. Add assertions for status, response schema, and that 'token' is non-empty.

TODO (candidate): remove hard-coded Authorization header — handle it at the collection level. Assert that returned user_id matches the logged-in user. Capture user_id into {{user_id}} for downstream requests.
TODO (candidate): parameterize room_id from env. Assert the user appears in the room's audience list after joining (chain a follow-up GET if needed). Assert idempotent behavior on a second join.

TODO (candidate): generate a fresh UUID v4 for idempotency_key in a pre-request script (Postman has pm.variables.replaceIn('{{$guid}}') or you can use crypto). Capture the pre-call balance in a previous request, then assert post-call balance = pre - (quantity * gift.coin_price). Validate the 'winners' array length matches winners_count.

exec: // TODO (candidate): add a collection-level pre-request script here that
// auto-attaches Authorization: Bearer {{auth_token}} to every request
// TODO (candidate): add any collection-level test script here that runs
// after every request (e.g., assert response time, common header checks).
